originalTitle: In Madrid fans defy heat, early starts and the torpor of a slow game to cheer Spain to World Cup victory
summary: With the temperature at 9pm close to 32C, Spanish fans filled bars, parks and arena’s to celebrate their team’s 1-0 win over Argentina
content: Neither the approach of yet another enervating heatwave, nor the prospect of heading to work on Monday morning, was going to keep madrileños huddled indoors when the World Cup, and the stifling streets of the capital, were there for the taking. With the temperature at 9pm nudging 32C, people across the city and the wider region filled bars, parks, fairgrounds and bullrings to drink, to stripe the streets red-and-yellow with their rojigualda flags, and to test their lungs, their nerves, and, as it turned out, their boredom thresholds. In the capital itself, the faithful descended on a giant screen by the river Manzanares and trooped into the cavernous concrete confines of the 15,000-capacity Movistar arena. Most, however, appeared to have made for the Plaza de Colón to watch the game under the statue of Christopher Columbus, for whom the square is named. Overhead, sharp against a bright blue July sky, flew the largest flag in Spain: a vexillological behemoth that measures 21m by 14m and weighs 35kg. While the plaza frequently doubles as a tournament-screening venue, the fact that Spain were playing one of their former imperial territories under the watchful eye of Columbus added a certain undeniable piquancy. The Argentinian ambassador in Madrid had alluded to the two countries’ long, shared history in a video message over the weekend in which he had urged pride and cool heads. “What a dream of a final: Spain and Argentina,” said Wenceslao Bunge. “Two brother countries. Two countries that have so very much in common … Let this final be an example for us and for the world when it comes sport and to the meaning of values such as work, perseverance and respect for others.” On Sunday night, the brother countries certainly had one thing in common – and it all depended on what happened on a pitch in faraway New Jersey. La Roja were hoping for another historic victory 16 years after Andrés Iniesta’s 116th-minute goal against the Netherlands in South Africa gave Spain their first World Cup triumph. Meanwhile La Albiceleste – who last lifted the trophy in 2022 after beating France on penalties in Qatar – were keen to secure a fourth title. Just before the game began amid hopeful cries of “a por la segunda!” (Let’s get a second title!), Sebastián Eros, a Chilean who stood with a Spain flag politely tied around his neck, admitted to being a little agnostic about the result. “We’re not really bothered who wins,” he confessed. “If Spain win, then that’s brilliant. But if Argentina win – even though they can be a pain in the arse – then at least the cup will stay in South America.” A 18-year-old madrileña called Katy was rather more psyched. “It’s a historic moment and it’s really exciting,” said Katy, who was two years old the last time Spain won the World Cup. Miguel Hinojal Vicente, 37, had come to Madrid from Toledo as much for the party atmosphere as for the match itself. Unlike Katy, he had vivid memories of Spain’s last World Cup triumph. “Shit, it was just a magical thing,” he said. “But, to tell the truth, I’m more of an Atléti fan than a Spain fan,” referring to Atlético Madrid football club. His friend Raquel García was a little more enthusiastic – and optimistic. “This is about the hopes of a country and it’s about unity,” she said. “Events like these bring people together and they make people happy.” For an eternity, however, it looked as though no one would ever be happy. The slow pace of the game – coupled with the lingering heat, the drifts of dope smoke and the soporific drones of vuvuzelas – loosed an atmosphere of fidgety boredom that wasn’t broken until 11.26pm, when Nico Williams slammed the ball into the Argentinian goal – only for the goal to be disallowed. The roars gave way to a disappointed torpor and some of the more refreshed fans began to nod off in doorways. They were rudely awakened 17 minutes later, when Ferran Torres’ goal drew a thunderous roar from the crowd. Screams, smiles and flares erupted and suddenly everything had become very much worth the agonising dull wait. Another disallowed goal for Spain may have followed but by then, La Roja’s momentum had become unstoppable. At the final whistle, which rang out a little after midnight, everyone in the Plaza de Colón – fans, police, ambulance workers – howled with delight. Tears flowed and hugs were suddenly mandatory. A por la segunda? Si señor!
chineseTitle: 在马德里，球迷们不畏炎热、早早开始和缓慢比赛的迟钝，为西班牙队赢得世界杯加油
chineseSummary: 晚上 9 点气温接近 32 摄氏度，西班牙球迷挤满酒吧、公园和竞技场庆祝球队 1-0 战胜阿根廷
chineseContent: 当世界杯和首都令人窒息的街道即将来临之际，无论是另一场令人衰弱的热浪的逼近，还是周一早上上班的前景，都不会让马德里人挤在室内。晚上 9 点气温逼近 32 摄氏度，整个城市和更广泛地区的人们涌入酒吧、公园、露天市场和斗牛场喝酒，用 rojigualda 旗帜将街道染成红黄相间，测试他们的肺部、神经，以及测试他们的无聊阈值。在首都，信徒们降落在曼萨纳雷斯河畔的巨大屏幕上，然后成群结队地走进可容纳 15,000 人的 Movistar 体育场的巨大混凝土空间。然而，大多数人似乎是前往科隆广场在克里斯托弗·哥伦布雕像下观看比赛，该广场就是以哥伦布的名字命名的。头顶上，在七月明亮的蓝色天空的映衬下，西班牙最大的旗帜飘扬着：一面旗帜巨兽，尺寸为 21m x 14m，重 35kg。虽然该广场经常兼作比赛放映场地，但西班牙在哥伦布的注视下在其前帝国领土之一进行比赛这一事实增添了某种不可否认的刺激性。阿根廷驻马德里大使在周末的一段视频中提到了两国悠久的共同历史，他在视频中敦促人们保持自豪感和冷静的头脑。 “多么梦幻的决赛：西班牙和阿根廷，”文塞斯劳·邦格说道。 “两个兄弟国家。两个有很多共同点的国家……让这场决赛成为我们和世界在体育方面以及工作、坚持和尊重他人等价值观的意义方面的榜样。”周日晚上，兄弟国家当然有一个共同点——而这一切都取决于什么
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
